$schema: This is descriptive research evidence, not normative doctrine
note: All content is extracted from research sources. This does NOT assign Prisma colors to films or create rankings. These are case studies for editorial reference only.

films.title: Red Desert
films.color_discussion_summary: Antonioni's first color film. Did not trust 'reality' of Technicolor. Literally painted grass, fruit, and streets grey to achieve grey desolation of industrial landscape. Color used to project protagonist's (Giuliana) internal neurosis onto external world. When anxious, world looks poisonous yellow. When numb, it is grey. Color is not atmospheric; it is structural.
films.key_insight: Color is structural, not atmospheric. Physical world painted to match psychological state.

sources.description: Analysis of Antonioni's color methodology in Red Desert

films.title: Three Colors: Blue
films.color_discussion_summary: Blue is relentless - filters, swimming pools, candy wrappers, rooms. Kieślowski claimed colors were not 'symbolic' in rigid sense (Liberty), but rather atmospheric. Blue represents 'trap' of memory Julie cannot escape. Use of blue is melancholic but lucid. Distinct from 'Cold Blue' of horror or 'Romantic Blue' of moonlight. It is 'Grief Blue' - heavy, aqueous, and suffocating.
films.key_insight: Blue not symbolic but atmospheric. Represents inescapable memory. Grief Blue - heavy, aqueous, suffocating.

sources.description: Director's clarification that colors are atmospheric, not rigidly symbolic

films.title: In the Mood for Love
films.color_discussion_summary: Film bathed in warmth - red curtains, gold wallpapers, amber streetlights. But 'green' of corridors cuts through, creating sense of staleness or decay. Colors function as 'Time Capsule.' Mimic look of faded photographs or specific chemical makeup of 60s decor. Saturation implies intensity of repressed emotion. 'Hot' film about 'cold' behavior (restraint).
films.key_insight: Colors mimic faded photographs and 60s chemical decor. Saturation = intensity of repressed emotion. Hot visuals, cold behavior.

sources.description: Describes color as emotionally expressive, tied to desire, repetition, and memory

films.title: Moonlight
films.color_discussion_summary: Blue stripped of its 'cold' connotation. Under moonlight, black skin rendered with luminous quality that signals intimacy and safety. Blue is only place protagonist can be his true self. Inverts traditional Warm = Happy, Cool = Sad color theory.
films.key_insight: Blue becomes warm and intimate. Counter to traditional color psychology. Blue = safe space for true self.

sources.description: Analysis of inverted color meaning in Moonlight

films.title: Midsommar
films.color_discussion_summary: Weaponizes 'Happy' palette. Uses blinding white sunlight, pastel flowers, and lush greens to signify terror. Horror comes from lack of shadows - 'exposure' to light. Inversion creates cognitive dissonance; eyes see 'Paradise,' mind registers 'Threat.'
films.key_insight: Happy palette weaponized. No shadows = exposure = terror. Eyes see paradise, mind registers threat.

films.title: Mandy
films.color_discussion_summary: Operates in two color modes: The Edenic (soft, grainy, golden-hour warmth) and The Hellish (deep, monochromatic Magnesium Violet and Blood Red). Used practical lighting effects (moving lights, flares) to create texture that feels 'wood-grained' and organic, not flat digital wash. Color shifts signal break in reality - descent into 'Cosmic Void.' Texture as Horror.
films.key_insight: Two distinct color modes signal reality states. Practical effects create organic texture. Monochromatic nightmare vs. edenic warmth.

sources.description: Loeb on practical lighting effects and texture

films.title: The Florida Project
films.color_discussion_summary: Poverty usually depicted in desaturated greys. Baker depicts it in 'Blueberry Ice Cream' Purple and Bubblegum Pink. Palette reflects child's perspective (Moonee). To her, cheap motels are magic kingdom. Tragedy arises from dissonance between 'Candy' visuals and 'Grim' reality. Shot on 35mm for 'Summer Memory' texture.
films.key_insight: Poverty shown in candy colors reflecting child's perspective. Dissonance between visual joy and narrative tragedy creates emotional impact.

sources.description: Analysis of color as perspective device

films.title: Timbuktu
films.color_discussion_summary: Oppressors (Jihadists) visually linked to beige/grey desert and black flags. Resistance linked to Color. In 'Fish Seller' scene, vibrant colors of woman's wrap and fish visually disrupt monochromatic authoritarianism. Color becomes symbol of Life and Defiance. Cinematography uses blinding 'white heat' of sun to wash out world, making bursts of color more powerful.
films.key_insight: Color as political resistance. Vibrant hues disrupt monochromatic authoritarianism. Desaturation = oppression, saturation = life.

sources.description: Analysis of color as political symbolism

films.title: Vertigo
films.color_discussion_summary: Use of Green (Judy's dress, neon sign outside hotel) is color of 'Ghost' (Madeleine). Hitchcock uses green as 'fog' that obscures reality. Color of Scottie's obsession. In Technicolor world of vibrant reds and blues, sickly neon green stands out as 'Unnatural' and 'Undead.'
films.key_insight: Green as uncanny, unnatural color. Fog that obscures reality. Color of obsession and the undead.

sources.description: Analysis of Hitchcock's use of green in Vertigo

films.title: Apocalypse Now
films.color_discussion_summary: Storaro used artificial colored smoke in Vietnam. Rejection of naturalism in favor of operatic symbolism. Represents conflict through color temperature opposition (Nature vs. Technology).
films.key_insight: Artificial colored smoke for symbolic effect. Color as conflict between forces.

films.title: Carol
films.color_discussion_summary: Super 16mm chosen to emulate Ektachrome slide film - specifically its tendency to turn greens cool and magentas mute. 'Media Accurate' rather than 'Period Accurate.' By replicating deficiencies of 1950s media, created more potent emotional connection than technically perfect digital image. Texture + Color = Time.
films.key_insight: Memory of 1950s (cool greens, magentas) prioritized over reality. Film stock deficiencies create emotional authenticity.

sources.description: Discusses choice of Super 16mm and Ektachrome emulation

films.title: Blade Runner 2049
films.color_discussion_summary: Extreme orange of Las Vegas grounded in diegetic source (radiation/sandstorms). 'Justified stylization' - even extreme color must have narrative motivation. Creates monochromatic night environments that feel stylized yet physically plausible.
films.key_insight: Extreme stylization grounded in narrative logic. Monochromatic environments physically plausible despite visual intensity.

sources.description: Discusses approach to color and justification

films.title: The Godfather
films.color_discussion_summary: Exemplifies 1970s 'New Hollywood' grit aesthetic. Driven by Eastman Kodak stocks that could be 'pushed' in development. 'Muddy Realism' - earth tones (browns, greens, ochres) dominated. Shadows allowed to go black or noisy green. Grain as texture of authenticity. Palette signaled rejection of 'fake' studio system, represented 'Truth,' 'Grime,' and 'Corruption.'
films.key_insight: Earth tones and grain signaled authenticity. Rejection of studio artifice. Shadows go black or noisy green.

sources.description: Analysis of 1970s film stock and push processing

films.title: Black Narcissus
films.color_discussion_summary: Three-strip Technicolor process defined mid-century visual imagination. Required immense light (arc lamps), necessitating shadows be filled with colored light rather than left black. 'Operatic Saturation' - colors distinct, separated, vibrant. Red did not bleed into Blue. Signals 'Movie Magic' rather than 'Documentary Truth.'
films.key_insight: Technicolor process created specific aesthetic memory of era. Shadows filled with colored light. Distinct, separated colors.

sources.description: Analysis of three-strip Technicolor process and Cardiff's work

films.title: Good Time
films.color_discussion_summary: 'Neon-Noir' aesthetic. Uses colors human eye perceives as 'chemical' or 'toxic' - acid greens, blacklight purples, sodium vapor oranges. Colors signal altered state of consciousness, distinct from 'romantic' neon of 1980s. Aggressive, vibrating, anxious. Mixed temperatures (orange streetlights, blue moon, green fluorescents) in same frame without correction.
films.key_insight: Colors perceived as toxic or chemical. Signal altered state. Mixed temperatures uncorrected. Aggressive and anxious.

films.title: Spring Breakers
films.color_discussion_summary: 'Neon-Noir' aesthetic with toxic neon palette. Acid greens, blacklight purples. Colors signal altered consciousness. Aggressive, vibrating colors distinct from romantic 1980s neon.
films.key_insight: Colors as chemical perception. Signal dissociation and altered states.

films.title: Touki Bouki
films.color_discussion_summary: Mentioned in context of 'Post-Colonial' use of color where palette disrupts narrative rather than supporting it. Color as standalone text operating independently of dialogue.
films.key_insight: Color disrupts rather than supports narrative. Operates as independent text.

sources.description: Analysis of post-colonial cinema and color
